facts.label: THE BORROWED YELLOW
facts.story: Argentina landed in Sweden for the 1958 World Cup without a proper alternate kit. When they faced West Germany in Malmö and both sides showed up in white, there was no time to improvise. Local club IFK Malmö lent their own yellow shirts, and Argentina played an entire World Cup match in someone else's colors, losing 3–1 in yellow they'd never worn before or since.

facts.label: 38 SHIRTS FROM A MARKET
facts.story: Three days before facing England in the 1986 quarter-final, coach Carlos Bilardo wanted lighter blue shirts from supplier Le Coq Sportif, but they couldn't deliver in time. A staffer scoured Mexico City for 38 plain shirts instead, and the crew hand-stitched the AFA crest onto each one and ironed on silvery American-football numbers. Diego Maradona wore one of those makeshift jerseys to score the Hand of God and the Goal of the Century in the same afternoon.

1930.headline: Stábile's Golden Debut
1930.story: Guillermo Stábile, a late replacement, scored a hat-trick against Mexico and finished as the tournament's top scorer with eight goals in his only World Cup. Argentina routed the United States 6–1 in the semi-final, then blew a 2–1 halftime lead in the inaugural final, falling 4–2 to host Uruguay at the Estadio Centenario.

annotations.label: STÁBILE'S HAT-TRICK DEBUT
annotations.story: Guillermo Stábile was a late replacement, uncapped before the tournament began. He scored a hat-trick against Mexico in his international debut and finished as the World Cup's top scorer with eight goals — all of it in the only tournament he ever played for Argentina.

annotations.label: NUMBERED BY THE ALPHABET
annotations.story: Squad numbers were still a novelty in 1930 — players were numbered 1 through 22 by their spot on the official roster list, not by playing position, and wore that same number for every match. Argentina's shirts carried no crest, no sponsor, just plain sky-blue-and-white stripes.

annotations.label: CROSSING THE RIVER PLATE
annotations.story: Most European federations balked at the three-week boat trip to Uruguay, leaving a mostly South American field for the inaugural tournament. Thousands of Argentine supporters crossed the Río de la Plata by ferry to Montevideo for the final, only to sail home after a tense 4–2 loss to the hosts.

1930.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks

imageCredit.game: Argentina national team, 1930 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

matches.scorers: Stábile 8', Zumelzú 12', Stábile 17', Varallo 53', Zumelzú 55', Stábile 80'
Monti 20', Scopelli 56', Stábile 69', Peucelle 80', Peucelle 85', Stábile 87'

1934.headline: One and Done in Italy
1934.story: A dispute over amateur versus professional players gutted Argentina's roster before they even reached Italy. Ernesto Belis and Alberto Galateo scored, but Sweden eliminated them in the very first match, 3–2, sending Argentina home after just one game under the tournament's straight-knockout format.

annotations.label: ONE MATCH, THEN HOME
annotations.story: A rift over paying amateur players stripped Argentina's roster before they'd even landed in Italy. Ernesto Belis and Alberto Galateo both scored against Sweden, but the tournament's single-match knockout format meant a 3–2 loss ended Argentina's World Cup after just one game.

annotations.label: NO CREST NO BADGE
annotations.story: Argentina's shirts in Italy were still bare cotton stripes — no shield, no stars, no manufacturer's mark. The AFA wouldn't add its now-famous badge for another 24 years, meaning the jersey that lost to Sweden looked almost identical to the one worn at the very first World Cup in 1930.

annotations.label: MUSSOLINI'S PROPAGANDA SHOWCASE
annotations.story: Benito Mussolini treated the tournament as a stage for Fascist Italy, packing stadiums with Blackshirts and leaning on officials to favor the host nation. Argentina, gutted by its dispute over paying amateur players, arrived under-strength into that atmosphere and lasted one match before the long boat ride home.

1934.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks

imageCredit.game: Argentina squad photo, 1934 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

1958.headline: Borrowed Yellow, Beaten Blue
1958.story: Forced into local club IFK Malmö's yellow shirts against West Germany after arriving without an alternate kit, Argentina lost 3–1. Czechoslovakia then routed them 6–1 in Helsingborg, still the heaviest defeat in the team's history, and Argentina went out after the group stage.

annotations.label: HEAVIEST DEFEAT EVER
annotations.story: Already eliminated after two matches, Argentina met Czechoslovakia in Helsingborg and collapsed 6–1, still the biggest loss in the national team's World Cup history. Omar Corbatta's consolation goal in the 65th minute was the only bright spot in a tournament already lost.

annotations.label: THE SHIELD ARRIVES
annotations.story: Argentina's players wore an official crest for the first time at this tournament — a sky-blue shield bearing the federation's initials, still without the laurel wreath or stars that would come later. It's the same badge, twice evolved since, that sits on every Argentina jersey today.

annotations.label: THE AIRPORT RECEPTION
annotations.story: News of the 6–1 humiliation to Czechoslovakia reached Buenos Aires before the players did, and an angry crowd met the squad on arrival. Newspapers ran mock obituaries for Argentine football, and the disgrace pushed the AFA toward a wholesale overhaul of how the national team was coached and selected.

1958.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks
1958.awayColors: borrowed yellow IFK Malmö shirts (no alternate kit brought to Sweden)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs Czechoslovakia, 1958 FIFA World Cup Group Stage (Wikimedia Commons)

1962.headline: Stuck in Neutral in Chile
1962.story: A goalless draw with Hungary, sandwiched between a win over Bulgaria and a loss to England, left Argentina level on points but short on goal difference in windswept Rancagua. They exited after the first round for the second straight tournament running.

annotations.label: STUCK ON GOAL DIFFERENCE
annotations.story: A goalless draw with Hungary in Rancagua's wind left Argentina level on points with rivals in Group D. Only goal difference separated advancement from elimination, and Argentina came up short, exiting after the first round for a second straight World Cup.

annotations.label: STILL UNBRANDED STRIPES
annotations.story: A dozen years before Adidas signed on, Argentina's kit carried no manufacturer's logo at all — just heavy cotton stripes that soaked through in Rancagua's rain and wind. The shield badge introduced in 1958 remained the only branding on the shirt, sewn on by hand rather than woven in.

annotations.label: BUILT AFTER THE QUAKE
annotations.story: Chile hosted barely two years after the 1960 Valdivia earthquake, the most powerful ever recorded, which had flattened stadiums along with everything else. Argentina played its group matches at Rancagua's Estadio El Teniente, rebuilt specifically for the tournament as proof the country had recovered enough to host the world.

1962.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks

imageCredit.game: Argentina national team, 1962 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

1966.headline: Wembley's 'Animals' Remark
1966.story: Luis Artime scored three of Argentina's four goals to top a tight group, setting up a quarter-final against England at Wembley. Captain Antonio Rattín was sent off and refused to leave the pitch for several minutes, and England won 1–0 — a result manager Alf Ramsey later soured by calling Argentina's players 'animals.'

annotations.label: RATTÍN'S WEMBLEY STAND-OFF
annotations.story: Sent off in the quarter-final against England at Wembley, captain Antonio Rattín refused to leave the pitch, delaying the match for several minutes before finally walking off past the Royal Box. England won 1–0, a result manager Alf Ramsey later soured by calling Argentina's players 'animals.'

annotations.label: THE LAST HOMEMADE KIT
annotations.story: England '66 was Argentina's final World Cup before Adidas took over kit production in 1974. The shirts were still tailored domestically, plain sky-blue-and-white stripes with the AFA shield and no outside sponsor, a look that would seem almost quaint next to the branded strips of the following decade.

annotations.label: THE 'ANIMALS' FALLOUT
annotations.story: Alf Ramsey's comment ignited a genuine diplomatic spat — Argentine newspapers ran furious editorials, the AFA lodged formal protests with FIFA, and Britain's ambassador in Buenos Aires had to publicly distance himself from the remark. The insult calcified into one of football's most enduring national rivalries.

1966.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs West Germany, 1966 FIFA World Cup Group Stage (Wikimedia Commons)

1974.headline: Cruyff's Netherlands Ends It
1974.story: René Houseman's three goals carried Argentina through an uneven first group, but the second-round group brought Johan Cruyff's Netherlands, who dismantled them 4–0 in Gelsenkirchen. Face-saving draws with East Germany and Brazil closed out a tournament that never got out of second gear.

annotations.label: HOUSEMAN CARRIES THE GROUP
annotations.story: René Houseman scored three goals across Argentina's uneven first-round group, including two against Haiti, to help the team scrape through the pool. It wasn't enough momentum: the second round brought Johan Cruyff's Netherlands, who dismantled Argentina 4–0 in Gelsenkirchen days later.

annotations.label: ADIDAS ERA BEGINS
annotations.story: West Germany '74 marked the first time Argentina wore an Adidas-made kit, launching a partnership that has now lasted five decades. Gone was the hand-tailored look of previous tournaments, replaced by the brand's three stripes down the sleeve, the same template that would carry Argentina to its first title four years later.

annotations.label: PERÓN'S BRIEF RETURN
annotations.story: Argentina traveled to West Germany just months after Juan Perón returned from 18 years of exile to reclaim the presidency, only to die in office weeks before the tournament ended. The team came home to a country already sliding toward the political violence that would define the rest of the decade.

1974.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (first Adidas kit)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs East Germany, 1974 FIFA World Cup 2nd Round — CC BY-SA 3.0 DE / Bundesarchiv, Wikimedia Commons

1978.headline: Kempes Wins It at Home
1978.story: Hosting under a military dictatorship, Argentina needed a heavily disputed 6–0 rout of Peru to reach the final ahead of Brazil on goal difference. Mario Kempes scored twice in extra time of the final, and Daniel Bertoni added a third as Argentina beat the Netherlands 3–1 at the Monumental for their first title.

annotations.label: KEMPES DECIDES IT AT HOME
annotations.story: Playing under a military dictatorship and a wall of home noise at the Monumental, Mario Kempes scored twice in extra time of the final against the Netherlands. Daniel Bertoni added a third in a 3–1 win that gave Argentina its first World Cup title.

annotations.label: SHIELD EARNS ITS STAR
annotations.story: Argentina's badge had carried its laurel wreath for years but no stars — there had never been a title to mark. After Kempes lifted the trophy at the Monumental, the AFA added a single gold star above the shield, the first of what are now three, stitched onto every shirt since.

annotations.label: SHADOWS OVER THE MONUMENTAL
annotations.story: Blocks from the stadium where Argentina lifted the trophy, the ESMA naval school operated as one of the dictatorship's largest clandestine detention centers, where thousands of political prisoners were held and killed. The World Cup's images of celebration coexisted with atrocities the regime worked hard to keep hidden from foreign cameras.

1978.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs Netherlands, 1978 FIFA World Cup Final (Wikimedia Commons)

matches.scorers: Kempes 21', Tarantini 43', Kempes 49', Luque 50', Houseman 67', Luque 72'

1982.headline: Golden Boy Silenced by Italy
1982.story: A 20-year-old Diego Maradona wore Argentina's number 10 at his first World Cup, but the defending champions lost their opener to Belgium and never recovered. Claudio Gentile fouled Maradona out of the second-round group decider, and Italy's 2–1 win sent Argentina home before the semi-finals.

annotations.label: GENTILE MARKS MARADONA OUT
annotations.story: Claudio Gentile shadowed and fouled a 20-year-old Diego Maradona relentlessly through the second-round decider against Italy, neutralizing the tournament's most-watched newcomer. Italy won 2–1, and the defending champions were eliminated before the semi-finals for the first time since 1966.

annotations.label: LE COQ TAKES OVER
annotations.story: Spain '82 was the first World Cup where Argentina wore Le Coq Sportif instead of Adidas, the French brand's rooster logo replacing the three stripes for what became a seven-year run. The switch coincided with the team's worst title defense in tournament history, though the two were entirely unrelated.

annotations.label: A WAR JUST ENDED
annotations.story: Argentina's military junta surrendered the Falklands War to Britain on 14 June 1982, one day after the World Cup kicked off in Spain. British broadcasters muted commentary on Argentina's matches, and the defending champions played most of the group stage as a nation formally still at war.

1982.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Le Coq Sportif)

imageCredit.game: Argentina supporters, 1982 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

1986.headline: Hand of God, Then the Cup
1986.story: Diego Maradona scored the illegal 'Hand of God' and the mazy 'Goal of the Century' seven minutes apart against England, wearing a jersey improvised in a Mexico City market. He set up Jorge Burruchaga's winner in the final, beating West Germany 3–2 for Argentina's second title.

annotations.label: HAND OF GOD, GOAL OF THE CENTURY
annotations.story: Seven minutes apart against England in the quarter-final, Diego Maradona punched in an illegal opener, then beat five defenders on a mazy dribble for arguably the greatest solo goal in World Cup history. Argentina won 2–1 en route to a second title.

annotations.label: A TRICOLOR TRIM
annotations.story: Away from the improvised market shirts of the England game, Argentina's regular Le Coq Sportif kit for Mexico '86 featured a thin tricolor trim on the collar and cuffs, the French brand's signature detail. It's the shirt Maradona wore to lift the trophy, not the makeshift one from the quarter-final.

annotations.label: A HOST STILL REBUILDING
annotations.story: Just eight months before kickoff, a magnitude-8.0 earthquake killed thousands in Mexico City and damaged parts of the very stadiums the World Cup would use. Mexico kept its hosting rights anyway, becoming the first country to host the tournament twice, and Argentina lifted the trophy in a capital still clearing rubble.

1986.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Le Coq Sportif)
1986.awayColors: plain white shirts with hand-stitched AFA crest (worn vs. England, Quarter-final)

imageCredit.game: Diego Maradona with the World Cup trophy, Estadio Azteca, 1986 FIFA World Cup Final (Wikimedia Commons)

1990.headline: Two Red Cards in Rome
1990.story: Argentina scraped through a shaky group and needed penalties in both the quarter-final and semi-final. Pedro Monzón became the first player ever sent off in a World Cup final, joined minutes later by Gustavo Dezotti, as West Germany's Andreas Brehme converted a disputed late penalty for a 1–0 win.

annotations.label: TWO RED CARDS IN THE FINAL
annotations.story: Pedro Monzón became the first player ever sent off in a World Cup final, and Gustavo Dezotti joined him minutes later. Andreas Brehme converted a disputed late penalty, and West Germany won 1–0 in a bad-tempered rematch of the 1986 final.

annotations.label: ADIDAS RETURNS
annotations.story: After eight years with Le Coq Sportif, Adidas won back Argentina's kit contract for Italia '90, restoring the three stripes that had been absent since the 1982 title defense collapsed. The reunion produced one of the least memorable shirts of the partnership, worn to the ugliest final in tournament history.

annotations.label: HYPERINFLATION AT HOME
annotations.story: Argentina's players landed in Italy as their country endured hyperinflation exceeding 3,000 percent a year, prices changing by the hour and savings wiped out under outgoing president Raúl Alfonsín. Carlos Menem had already been elected on promises of stability, inheriting a crisis the team's run to the final briefly distracted the nation from.

1990.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

1994.headline: Maradona's Positive Test
1994.story: Diego Maradona scored and celebrated wild-eyed into a pitchside camera against Greece, then tested positive for ephedrine after the Nigeria game and was sent home in disgrace. Without their captain, Argentina lost 3–2 to Romania in the Round of 16.

annotations.label: MARADONA'S WILD-EYED FAREWELL
annotations.story: After scoring against Greece, Diego Maradona sprinted to a pitchside camera and screamed into the lens, eyes bulging — his last World Cup goal. He tested positive for ephedrine days later and was sent home in disgrace, never playing for Argentina again.

annotations.label: DIAMONDS ON THE SHIRT
annotations.story: Argentina's away kit for USA '94 broke from decades of plain stripes: navy blue scattered with black diamond flecks and a black polo collar, part of Adidas's maximalist 'Equipment' line worn across the brand's whole roster that summer. It remains one of the most collected Argentina jerseys ever reissued.

annotations.label: SCHEDULED FOR AMERICAN TV
annotations.story: USA '94 was built around American television slots rather than player comfort, forcing Argentina through group matches kicking off in early-afternoon heat and humidity across Texas and Massachusetts. FIFA introduced mandatory water breaks for the first time, a concession to conditions few European or South American squads had faced at a World Cup.

1994.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina match kit, 1994 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

1998.headline: Batistuta's Hat-Trick, Bergkamp's Strike
1998.story: Gabriel Batistuta became the first player to score hat-tricks at two different World Cups, burying three against Jamaica. Ariel Ortega's red card for headbutting the goalkeeper undid Argentina in the quarter-final, where Dennis Bergkamp's touch-turn-volley in the 89th minute beat them 2–1.

annotations.label: BERGKAMP'S 89TH-MINUTE TOUCH
annotations.story: Tied 1–1 in the quarter-final against the Netherlands, Dennis Bergkamp controlled a long ball out of the air, dragged it past a defender, and volleyed it home in the 89th minute. Ariel Ortega's red card for headbutting the goalkeeper had already left Argentina down to ten men.

annotations.label: REEBOK TAKES OVER
annotations.story: France '98 marked a rare break from Adidas: Reebok won the kit contract, swapping the three stripes for its own vector logo on Argentina's chest for a single tournament. It didn't last — Adidas reclaimed the deal within a few years, making the Reebok strip one of the shortest-lived kits in team history.

annotations.label: THE PONYTAIL RULE
annotations.story: Manager Daniel Passarella enforced a strict no-earrings, no-long-hair policy on the squad, a rule so rigid it kept the mulleted, earringed Claudio Caniggia off the World Cup roster entirely despite his talent. The disciplinarian approach split opinion inside Argentina long before a ball was kicked in France.

1998.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

2002.headline: Golden Generation, Group Exit
2002.story: A star-studded squad built around Batistuta, Verón, and Crespo lost their opener to David Beckham's penalty against England, then could only draw Sweden despite dominating possession. Argentina's so-called 'golden generation' went out in the group stage, one of the tournament's biggest upsets.

annotations.label: GOLDEN GENERATION GOES HOME
annotations.story: Built around Batistuta, Verón, and Crespo, Argentina lost their opener to David Beckham's penalty and could only draw Sweden despite dominating possession. The so-called golden generation crashed out in the group stage, one of the tournament's biggest upsets.

annotations.label: SHOULDER STRIPES ON NAVY
annotations.story: Argentina's away kit for the 2002 World Cup ditched the Reebok-era design entirely: a navy-blue shirt with light-blue-and-white stripes angled across the shoulders and patterned side panels, an Adidas template shared with several other national teams that summer in Japan and South Korea.

annotations.label: PLAYING THROUGH THE COLLAPSE
annotations.story: Argentina's golden generation traveled to Asia only months after the country's catastrophic 2001 economic collapse, which froze bank accounts, wiped out savings, and cycled through five presidents in two weeks. Millions back home who'd lost everything still tuned in, hoping the team could offer one thing the government hadn't: a win.

2002.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina match shirt, 2002 FIFA World Cup (Wikimedia Commons)

2006.headline: 24 Passes, One Perfect Goal
2006.story: Esteban Cambiasso finished a 24-pass team move against Serbia and Montenegro in a 6–0 rout still shown as a passing masterclass. Germany eliminated Argentina on penalties in Berlin's quarter-final, Jens Lehmann's cheat-sheet on their takers proving decisive in a 4–2 shootout.

annotations.label: CAMBIASSO'S 24-PASS GOAL
annotations.story: Against Serbia and Montenegro, Esteban Cambiasso finished a 24-pass team move in a 6–0 rout, a goal still shown as a passing masterclass. Germany eliminated Argentina in the quarter-final on penalties, Jens Lehmann's cheat-sheet on their takers proving decisive in a 4–2 shootout.

annotations.label: A COOLER TIGHTER FIT
annotations.story: Adidas introduced its ClimaCool ventilation technology to Argentina's 2006 shirt, mesh panels built to move heat off the body during Germany's summer matches. It was a small but permanent shift in how national-team kits were engineered, treating the shirt as performance gear rather than just a branded uniform.

annotations.label: A RIVALRY RENEWED
annotations.story: The penalty shootout loss to Germany was the two countries' first World Cup knockout meeting since the bitter 1990 final, and it reopened a rivalry that would define the next two tournaments: Germany eliminating Argentina again in 2010, then beating them in the 2014 final at the Maracanã.

2006.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs Serbia and Montenegro, 2006 FIFA World Cup Group Stage (Wikimedia Commons)

matches.scorers: Rodríguez 6', Cambiasso 31', Rodríguez 41', Crespo 78', Tevez 84', Messi 88'

2010.headline: Diego's Team Dismantled
2010.story: Diego Maradona's Argentina rolled through the group stage and past Mexico, but Germany's counter-attacking machine humiliated them 4–0 in the Cape Town quarter-final. It remains Argentina's heaviest World Cup knockout defeat and effectively ended Maradona's brief, chaotic reign as head coach.

annotations.label: GERMANY'S COUNTER-ATTACK MASTERCLASS
annotations.story: Diego Maradona's Argentina rolled through the group stage unbeaten, but Germany's rapid counters dismantled them 4–0 in the Cape Town quarter-final. It remains Argentina's heaviest World Cup knockout defeat and effectively ended Maradona's brief run as head coach.

annotations.label: THE BLACK STRIP DEBUTS
annotations.story: Argentina wore a black away shirt at a World Cup for the first time in 2010, a stark departure meant to stand out against opponents in similar sky-blue tones. Adidas paired it with gold trim on the collar, a design that has resurfaced as an away option in nearly every tournament since.

annotations.label: THE VUVUZELA WORLD CUP
annotations.story: South Africa 2010 was the first World Cup on African soil, played partly at altitude in Johannesburg and accompanied by the constant drone of vuvuzela horns that players from every nation complained drowned out on-pitch communication. Broadcasters eventually added audio filters to cut the noise for television audiences.

2010.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs South Korea, 2010 FIFA World Cup Group Stage (Wikimedia Commons)

2014.headline: So Close With Messi
2014.story: Lionel Messi carried a functional Argentina to a fifth final, scoring four goals including a stunner against Iran. Mario Götze's extra-time volley in the 113th minute beat them 1–0 at the Maracanã, denying Messi the title that had eluded Maradona's generation too.

annotations.label: GÖTZE'S EXTRA-TIME WINNER
annotations.story: Lionel Messi scored four goals to drag a functional Argentina to the final, but Mario Götze's chested-and-volleyed finish in the 113th minute won it for Germany at the Maracanã. It was Argentina's third World Cup final loss, denying Messi the title Maradona once won.

annotations.label: THE LIGHTEST SHIRT YET
annotations.story: Argentina was one of only a handful of Adidas nations issued the 'adizero' template at Brazil 2014, a shirt built roughly 40 percent lighter than a standard jersey using laser-cut seams instead of stitching. Germany wore the same technology to the final, and beat Argentina in it.

annotations.label: FINAL ON RIVAL SOIL
annotations.story: Argentina played for the title in Brazil, the country most responsible for their inferiority complex in South American football, at the Maracanã, the same stadium where Brazil suffered its own World Cup trauma in 1950. Losing there, to Germany, added a second layer of historical sting to the defeat.

2014.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Germany vs Argentina, 2014 FIFA World Cup Final (Wikimedia Commons)

2018.headline: Kylian Mbappé Announces Himself
2018.story: A stumbling Argentina barely escaped the group on Marcos Rojo's late winner over Nigeria, then ran into a 19-year-old Kylian Mbappé, who scored twice and terrorized their back line in a 4–3 Round of 16 loss — the tournament's clearest signal that a new generation had arrived.

annotations.label: MBAPPÉ ANNOUNCES HIMSELF
annotations.story: A 19-year-old Kylian Mbappé scored twice and terrorized Argentina's back line in a 4–3 Round of 16 defeat, the clearest signal yet that a new generation had arrived. Marcos Rojo's late winner over Nigeria was the only thing that got Argentina this far.

annotations.label: BLACK STRIP FLECKED DESIGN
annotations.story: Argentina's 2018 away kit reworked the black strip introduced in 2010 with a subtle pattern of sky-blue and white flecks scattered across the fabric, visible only up close. It was the shirt worn in the win over Nigeria that kept Argentina's tournament alive by a single late goal.

annotations.label: THE PLAYERS TOOK OVER
annotations.story: After a chaotic loss to Croatia, senior players reportedly stripped manager Jorge Sampaoli of tactical control and picked the starting lineup themselves for the must-win Nigeria match. The mutiny worked well enough to survive the group stage, but the dysfunction resurfaced days later against France.

2018.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs France, 2018 FIFA World Cup Round of 16 (Wikimedia Commons)

2022.headline: Messi's Final Redemption
2022.story: An opening loss to Saudi Arabia nearly derailed Lionel Messi's last World Cup, but Argentina won seven straight, including a Julián Álvarez-fueled semi-final rout of Croatia. Messi scored twice in the final, Kylian Mbappé's hat-trick forced a 3–3 draw, and Argentina won the shootout 4–2 for a third star.

annotations.label: MESSI'S FINAL REDEMPTION
annotations.story: An opening loss to Saudi Arabia nearly ended Lionel Messi's last World Cup before it began. Argentina won seven straight afterward, and Messi scored twice in a 3–3 final that Mbappé's hat-trick forced to penalties, where Argentina won 4–2 for a third star.

annotations.label: PURPLE FOR EQUALITY
annotations.story: Argentina wore purple at a World Cup for the first time in 2022, a shade Adidas chose specifically to evoke gender-equality movements, layered with a faint Sol de Mayo graphic nodding to the national flag. It was made from Parley Ocean Plastic recovered from beaches and coastlines rather than virgin polyester.

annotations.label: FOOTBALL'S FIRST WINTER CUP
annotations.story: Qatar forced the World Cup out of its usual summer slot for the first time, moving it to November and December to avoid the Gulf's extreme heat, which meant European leagues paused mid-season and players arrived undercooked or exhausted rather than rested. Argentina's title run defied a preparation cycle every rival also had to navigate.

2022.homeColors: light blue and white vertical stripes, black shorts, white socks (Adidas)
2022.awayColors: purple shirt (debuted in a competitive match at this tournament)

imageCredit.game: Argentina vs Mexico, 2022 FIFA World Cup Group Stage (Wikimedia Commons)
